summary: I was born in Tehran, the capital city of Iran, on June 14th, 1963. Growing up there, I lived in a household with my parents who were both educators. Tehran was my home until I was about 14, before my family moved to Germany in 1977.

Iran is where my roots are. I was born and raised there until my early teens, and my father worked for the Department of Education in Iran. After a tragic loss in my family and my parents' retirement in 1977, we left Iran for Germany. Later, during the Iran–Iraq War, I was hesitant to return because of the risk of being drafted.

We moved to Frankfurt, Germany, in 1977 after my parents retired. I was in 8th grade and didn't speak any German at the time, but I stayed there for over four years and graduated high school in Frankfurt in 1981. It was a challenging but important phase of my life where I adapted to a new culture and language.

Germany became my home from 1977 to 1981, specifically in Frankfurt. Although I was treated like a foreigner, I learned the language and played soccer there. It was also where I was discovered by an American scout, which eventually led me to the United States.

The Iran–Iraq War was a significant reason I was reluctant to return to Iran after high school. My father wanted to go back, but I feared being drafted into the military during the war. This conflict influenced my decision to seek opportunities elsewhere, eventually leading me to Australia and then the United States.

Australia was suggested by my father as a place to start fresh after Germany, and we applied for immigrant status there. Although I never ended up moving to Australia, it was part of the plan before I got the opportunity to go to the United States to play college soccer.

entity: State University of New York at Plattsburgh
summary: The State University of New York at Plattsburgh was where I went to college in the United States. I was recruited there to play soccer thanks to an American scout who saw me play in Germany. Attending SUNY Plattsburgh helped me adjust to life in the U.S. and make friends through soccer.

I moved to the United States after being recruited to play college soccer and overcoming visa challenges related to the Iran hostage crisis. I arrived at John F. Kennedy International Airport and found the culture and lifestyle easier to adapt to than Germany. Eventually, I became a U.S. citizen, though I waited until I was 31 to apply due to concerns about dual citizenship.

I arrived at John F. Kennedy International Airport when I first came to the United States. Although I was confident in my English, I struggled initially to understand how fast Americans spoke. From JFK, I went straight to SUNY Plattsburgh to start my college and soccer journey.
